beats.text: Here's our complex vector z — entries one and i, stacked vertically.

First attempt: z-transpose times z. The entries multiply straight across — one times one plus i times i. That's one plus i-squared. i-squared equals negative one, so we get zero. A vector with zero length? That's wrong.
The fix: replace transpose with conjugate-transpose — z-Hermitian. The entries of z flip to their conjugates: one stays one, i becomes negative i.
Now multiply: one times one plus i times negative i. That's one plus negative i-squared — one plus one — equals two. A bracket appears showing the length: root two. Correct.
For complex vectors, always use the conjugate-transpose. Regular transpose breaks length. Hermitian saves it.
